acad_most_impact_comment: Ashank Bhandari’s approach stood out for the way he connected product features with real-life examples, sharp ideas, and engaging delivery. From the very first session to the last, he maintained complete control of the classroom while keeping everyone actively involved. Even small details - like asking students to wear the B-Suit for presentations, which everyone followed - reflected the discipline he instilled, something rarely seen in other faculty sessions. It clearly showed that it’s not just the students, but the teacher who sets the tone and creates a culture of discipline and professionalism.
acad_feedback_comment: Yes, the rapport between faculties and students still feels missing, and because of that, many students have already started regretting joining the PGP program. There is a noticeable drop in interest when it comes to attending classes, largely because of the repeated episodes of walkouts, emotional breakdowns, visible frustration, or long monologues that don’t feel engaging. While it’s understandable that such situations can lead to faculty frustration or anger, there is always a more constructive way to handle them. Strong communication, mutual respect, and a calmer approach to resolving issues would go a long way in rebuilding trust and creating a more positive classroom environment. When students feel heard and respected, and when classes are structured in a more engaging and solution-oriented manner, it becomes easier for everyone to stay motivated and involved. There is a reason why none of the students faced these issues with Ashank or Vipin - their teaching style, clarity of expectations, and ability to connect with the class created a natural sense of discipline and engagement without the need for conflict.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: I haven’t opted for it.
outclass_feedback_comment: The outclass activities after the dropshipping module have honestly felt like a complete waste of time. We’ve gone through multiple sessions, but even now, many students are still unclear about what they’re actually supposed to build or how to move forward. When, after so many hours, people are still asking what an MVP really is, it reflects the gap between what’s being taught and what students are actually taking away. A big part of this confusion comes from the way the sessions are framed. Saksham’s perspective is consistently from a VC lens, which makes sense given his background. However, that viewpoint often feels disconnected from the reality students are facing. He evaluates ideas the way an investor would, but students are the ones taking the risk, starting from zero, and trying to build something real. When the feedback stops at “this idea won’t work” or “think bigger,” without any concrete direction on what to do next, it leaves people more confused than before. There is little hand-holding or follow-up support, which is what most students actually need at this stage. Sessions like MOM tests or MVP workshops end up feeling extremely basic and repetitive, covering concepts that most students already understand at a surface level. The issue isn’t the topics themselves; it’s the lack of depth and application. There’s plenty of discussion around validating ideas, but almost no guidance on what happens after an idea is selected. How do you build the first version? How do you acquire your first users? How do you deal with the first setbacks or pivots? These are the practical questions students are struggling with, and they remain largely unanswered. As a result, the experience starts to feel like a loop of theoretical discussions without real execution support. Students attend, listen, and leave with the same doubts they walked in with. Over time, this builds frustration across the batch. The discontent around Saksham’s sessions is quite visible, many students feel disappointed, and this sentiment has only grown since the AnM session, especially because there hasn’t been any meaningful follow-up support from his side. What was expected to bring clarity and momentum has instead left many people feeling directionless.
careerprep_confidence_comment: I feel the resume feedback has been quite generic overall. To be fair, the sessions did help with tackling behavioral questions, pressure-testing responses, and identifying a few strong hooks. But over time, I’m starting to see less value in the AnMs themselves, and even the PRS sessions don’t feel as important or impactful as I initially expected.
careerprep_feedback_comment: Honestly, I’m not even sure what exactly they should do better, but the current mechanism does feel somewhat obsolete. Most resumes are already done, students aren’t very interested in creating video CVs, and many have shifted their focus toward building portfolios instead. Rather than pushing the same set of activities, it might be more useful if they spoke to students one-on-one to understand the actual problems they’re facing. For example, if students aren’t making video CVs, the first step should be to ask why, whether it’s lack of clarity, time constraints, confidence issues, or simply not seeing the value. Understanding the real blockers could help them design support that is more relevant and effective.
careerservices_feedback_comment: For many of us, our dream jobs still feel like just that—dreams. We aren’t seeing enough legacy or stable companies in the placement process, and as a result, some students feel pushed to apply for startup roles even when that wasn’t their original preference. This is one of the reasons the number of placed students so far is relatively low. People are gradually losing confidence-whether it’s anxiety or just uncertainty about the process, it’s definitely being felt across the batch. At this point, what most of us are really hoping for is the presence of stronger, more established companies on campus, so students can pursue roles that align better with their long-term goals and expectations.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: I don’t think we’ve had enough interaction with the career services team so far. Most of the engagement has been limited to status reviews, and that too only once in a quarter. It would be much more helpful if these sessions happened more frequently, perhaps once every month, and then every two weeks during the peak placement season. Regular check-ins like this could provide better guidance, keep everyone aligned, and help address concerns in a more timely manner.
masterrelations_feedback_comment: Yes, lately there are a few sessions where attendance feels forced. At the same time, for the sessions we genuinely want to attend, we do register, but then there’s a lot of chaos because students from multiple programs like UG, TETR, and SBM are all part of the same session. In that crowd, it often feels like we lose our individual value and attention. I’m not entirely sure what the right solution is, but this is something that definitely needs to be addressed.
studentaffairs_value_comment: I think the hostel party was one of the best things that happened, because it was genuinely needed to bring the batch together. It helped bridge the gap between Bricks and Flock, and slowly those differences are fading, especially as Brickspaze has also started getting more value and acceptance among students. However, the biggest disappointment for me was Unmute, which didn’t meet the expectations many of us had.
studentaffairs_feedback_comment: Events need to be planned and executed in a much more structured and engaging way. Right now, most of them feel either poorly communicated, rushed, or lacking any real incentive for students to participate. If the goal is to build energy and involvement, then the events have to feel worth our time - both in terms of experience and the reward system. There needs to be clearer planning, better communication, and rewards that actually motivate participation, not just token gestures. Also, speaking very honestly, I still don’t see a clear purpose or impact of the student affairs team in my overall experience so far. For a team that is supposed to enhance student life, there hasn’t been any noticeable contribution that has made a meaningful difference. If the team exists, its presence should be felt through better events, stronger engagement, and real problem-solving. Right now, that impact just isn’t visible, and that’s frustrating as a student who expected more from this function.
tech_careercoach_comment: They could consider adding more features that give students better visibility within the tool itself, for example, a clear status tracker for each application, an estimated probability of getting shortlisted, or other product features that help us understand where we stand. That kind of transparency would make the process feel more structured and less uncertain. That said, the teams have been quite supportive when it comes to resolving queries, and the turnaround time has been quick. So there are no complaints on that front, overall, the experience has been good so far.
tech_mentorunion_comment: No specific feedback as such, but on a general note, I’ve noticed that some mentor suggestions tend to go against the program’s own guidelines. For example, I asked one mentor to review my CV, and the feedback I received included changes that the career prep team had specifically advised us not to make. This creates confusion about which direction we’re actually supposed to follow.
tech_lms_comment: Nothing major, everything on the LMS is working fine. Just like mentioned before, Transparency of things will make things better.
program_nps_reason_comment: Positives: There has been a noticeable improvement in the academic side of the program. The structure and quality of classes have become better, and some of the recent sessions have felt more practical and relevant than before. The career team has also become more supportive over time. Their responsiveness has improved, and they are making more efforts to help students with applications, preparation, and queries. Another strong point has been the tutors—many of them come with solid industry experience, which reflects in their teaching. Their examples, case studies, and practical insights make the sessions more relatable and useful for real-world scenarios. Negatives: The Outclass experience has been quite disappointing overall. Many of the sessions have felt unstructured, repetitive, or too basic, without giving students clear direction on what to actually build or how to move forward. Instead of creating momentum, these sessions have often left students feeling more confused and disengaged. Additionally, there have been instances where classroom environments were affected by emotional reactions or visible frustration from faculty members. Situations involving walkouts, emotional moments, or expressions of frustration during sessions have made the atmosphere uncomfortable and distracting for students. While it’s understandable that faculty also face pressure, these reactions tend to impact the learning environment negatively. A more composed, structured, and solution-oriented approach would help maintain professionalism and create a better classroom experience for everyone.
program_positive_comment: Career placement transparency has been good, which helped students stay informed and reduced uncertainty around the process. The academic calendar also felt more relaxed and manageable, giving students better breathing room to balance academics, placements, and personal projects. Another positive change was the expectation to present academic work in B-suit formals. This felt appropriate for a business school environment and added a sense of professionalism that was previously missing. Additionally, the networking and party sessions were a good initiative. They gave the batch an opportunity to interact beyond classrooms, build stronger relationships, and create a more cohesive and engaging environment overall.
program_fix_comment: I feel that the TBM batch at MU is gradually losing its perceived value. With the introduction of multiple programs like UG, RISE, SBM, and CM, the overall number of students associated with MU has increased significantly, and TBM students now form a smaller proportion of that pool. Because of this, when TBM students are out in the market, it doesn’t feel as distinctive or prestigious to say we’re from MU, since there are now many others from different programs saying the same thing. For example, when someone says they are from ISB, the other person rarely asks whether it’s PGP, PGP Pro, PGP Max, or YLP—because the ISB brand itself carries that value. We want MU to reach a similar position, where the brand stands strong and commands respect in the market. One way to preserve that perception could be by being more selective with student intake or maintaining a clear distinction between programs, so the flagship offering doesn’t get diluted. TBM is the flagship program of MU, and it should continue to hold that position of importance and prestige. Right now, it sometimes feels like the focus is shifting more toward other programs like Tetr or UG, while TBM isn’t getting the same level of attention. It would be great to see more emphasis on strengthening and protecting the value of the flagship program.

acad_most_impact_comment: Rajat Mathur's lecture left a very deep impact on me. His diction, his style of delivery, and the stories as well as experiences, as well as the activities he brings in the class, are above and beyond the world. Probably the most excited for any lecture to wake up in the morning and show up. And truly, truly great lecture. Ashank Bhandari’s lectures were excellent. He came extremely prepared and had deep command over his content. His delivery and the overall classroom experience were genuinely outstanding. He made product management feel very human and relatable, and there was never a dull moment in his class. He was not what some people loosely label as an “entertainer,” a term often used by the "heads" to suggest that we students only enjoy surface-level engagement. Yet, every minute of his lecture was enjoyable because of the sheer value it delivered. It was rigorous, thoughtful, and meaningful, which made the learning experience far more rewarding than mere entertainment. Dr. Garima’s lectures were truly excellent. I enjoyed each and every moment in her class. She made macroeconomics fun, engaging, and highly interactive, and I genuinely looked forward to every lecture she taught. What I appreciated the most was her humility and openness to learning. If there was ever a gap in understanding or a topic she wanted to explore further, she was comfortable asking questions herself and learning alongside us. She always met us at our level first, understood our perspective, and then lifted the entire class together. An amazing professor in every sense.
acad_feedback_comment: There should probably be fewer RIDE sessions and more focused lectures, as students would likely benefit more from that balance. If certain professors cannot manage multiple roles effectively, it might be better for them to stick to what they do best, which is teaching. When that balance is not maintained, lectures often drift away from learning and turn into extended sessions of disappointment, criticism, and commentary on how poor the batch supposedly is. While I understand that this may come from a place of concern, repeatedly spending a significant portion of lectures berating students does far more harm than good and ends up demotivating everyone. Over time, it begins to feel less like guidance and more like an attempt to shame or scare students into performing, which rarely works. In this term, some lectures, particularly in market research, felt disengaging and unfocused, with more time spent highlighting our shortcomings than actually teaching the subject. It even appeared as though the instructor himself was uninterested or burdened by the class. Additionally, it was already known that several students were struggling with statistics, yet no revision or bridge sessions were conducted despite long breaks during the term. While peer-to-peer learning has its place, it fails when an entire cohort is weak and only a handful are confident. In such cases, everyone stagnates. What was missing throughout was compassion and structured academic support, and that absence affected the entire batch.

masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: Lectures conducted by Mr. Prabhu were excellent. I am not from a marketing or growth background, but I happened to attend one of his classes. His diction was sharp, his command over the content was evident, and he had strong control over the classroom. What stood out most was his patience, even when faced with poorly framed questions. At the same time, he knew when to steer the discussion back on track, which is essential in areas like customer engagement and growth. Despite my limited background, I was able to absorb only about 20–30 percent of the material, but even that made the experience thoroughly worthwhile. It was genuinely impressive.

program_positive_comment: The roster of professors this term was quite strong. It felt like the faculty was better vetted compared to the previous term, both in terms of content and teaching ability. The professors seemed more in control, and the lectures were noticeably more engaging. That said, there were one or two sessions of some professors that felt less like learning experiences and more like shaming exercises, which took away from the overall academic value.
program_fix_comment: If I were sitting in Pratham’s chair, the first thing I would fix is how classroom time is used. I would clearly separate learning-focused lectures from feedback or alignment sessions. Lectures should remain dedicated to teaching and skill-building, while concerns around discipline, effort, or expectations should be addressed separately. These feedback sessions should be conducted in smaller, closed groups rather than large auditoriums. In small groups, students are more likely to open up and share honest feedback. The insights from these sessions can then be synthesized at a cohort level for action. This format would lead to clearer communication, higher accountability, and a more constructive academic environment without compromising classroom learning.

careerprep_feedback_comment: The FGDs are a good initiative but instead of doing them in groups of 10, groups of 3-4 are more effective. careerservices_feedback_comment: 1. It seems like there are quite a few 0-1, 1-10 team member startups, esp in the AI space, but would like a balance in scale of businesses coming in. 2. I also strongly request it to make it a mandate for the team to collect feedback from recruiters when they reject a profile/ choose not to shortlist anyone. I've experienced this every single time - even after I ask for feedback and chase POCs for feedback I NEVER hear back. They tell me they will let me know and never do. There was one particular opportunity where I felt my profile was highly relevant but I never got feedback on why I was rejected. I also have a strong suspicion that my profile was never sent across (it might have been missed) because I usually always get a email confirmation even if I have emailed the POC instead of applying through Career Coach, but I did not that one time. I had also flagged this to the POC but never heard back on what went wrong. Please help me understand why we're getting rejected bec otherwise I dont know how to position my profile better for future. 3. Please allow the right to opt out of opportunities (without so much resistance) that we do not sign up for in the first place. While I absolutely appreciate the intent, since we were not given the option to know more about the organisation before the process starts, its natural that we might not be interested for various reasons - either the fit is not as good as the POC thought, the industry is not suitable to the candidate, etc. Each student will have their own guardrails and all I ask the team in these situations is that they respect our guardrails. I had a particular experience where the poc let me opt out but I was also told "my profile wont be prioritized in the future". It was also implied that my profile was shared as a favour of sorts because I was rejected in another processes the POC was handling for me. It's in the team's interest as well to share my profile when the opportunity is a good fit - why would my profile be "de-prioritized" because I opted out of an opportunity I had never signed up for?? This feels very unprofessional and unsupportive 4. Please provide a split between fixed, variable and esops when posting a job because so many openings only have blanket CTC in the posting with no understanding of what to expect realistically. Even if they do give a split, the range is SO BROAD. Almost 10-15L sometimes! PLEASE make it standard to share a breakdown of the CTC with a reasonable range (5L) for the fixed component because I have no idea what to expect with most jobs. If the fixed component is always going to be on the lower end, at least I can alter my expectations from my placement then. I also have reached out to POCs to get an understanding but I never get a proper understanding. 5. Given we only get a few hours for each opportunity shared, that's limited time to do our due dilligence before applying. THAT is the sole reason so many students apply in a hurry and then find out more about the org and want to back out then. If only we get AT LEAST 36 hours for EVERY opportunity, it would largely help in the quality of applications you receive. Right now the students are juggling classes, assignments, VIP, sometimes individual projects as well - when we get only a few hours, either opportunities are missed or applied to without proper research. Giving more time also allows us to reach out to alums/employees who work in the org which HELPS A LOT in my decision making process. Its given me more clarity on my decision to apply or not, more than anything else.

First, upgrading or maintaining the coffee machine would make a noticeable difference in daily campus comfort, while continuing initiatives like providing fruits which has been a great addition should be encouraged. Parking is another area that needs attention, as difficulty finding parking can delay students and add unnecessary stress. A clearer parking system or designated student spaces could help improve punctuality and convenience. For Term 4 students, I would introduce clearer pathways distinguishing between those focused on building ventures and those preparing for jobs so they can receive targeted guidance without feeling pressured to balance both directions at once. Lastly, while space utilization has generally been good, relocating TBM classes to Tower A could improve accessibility and overall flow. These changes would make the environment more supportive, structured, and aligned with student needs.

acad_feedback_comment: I believe that at the beginning of the term, every student should choose one company in a sector they are genuinely interested in and work on it throughout the program as if they were the founder. Across subjects such as finance, marketing, operations, strategy, and product, students would apply their learning directly to this chosen company and make real, integrated decisions across different business verticals. This approach would encourage ownership, long-term thinking, and accountability rather than fragmented academic understanding. Students would build deep domain knowledge, develop cross-functional expertise, and learn to connect concepts across disciplines instead of treating each subject in isolation. By the end of the program, they would not just have theoretical knowledge but a well-rounded understanding of how a business operates end-to-end. This would create a significant competitive edge over other B-school students, particularly in securing high-impact roles aligned with their career aspirations. If executed well, such a model could meaningfully differentiate Masters’ Union and position it as a globally distinctive institution in management education.

outclass_feedback_comment: I truly appreciate the effort and intent the Outclass team puts into creating these platforms for founders. The initiative itself is valuable and well-structured. That said, I wanted to share a small observation. It feels like a common evaluation framework is being applied across startups operating in very different sectors. Given that business models, growth cycles, capital intensity, and key success metrics vary significantly by industry, a more sector-specific evaluation lens might allow for fairer and more meaningful feedback. Additionally, for the recent alumni pitches, it may have been helpful to categorize startups by sector and align them with alumni who have relevant domain experience. This could enable more nuanced judging and actionable advice, and ultimately create greater value for the founders presenting. Sharing this purely as constructive feedback with the intent of strengthening an already promising initiative.

program_fix_comment: 1. Deeper Leadership Engagement with Serious Builders I would aim to be more actively involved in key initiatives, particularly VIP activities. In addition, I would consider opening my calendar at least once a month for 1:1 conversations with teams that are genuinely committed to building. The Outclass team could help identify and filter serious teams based on clear parameters. Direct engagement from leadership can significantly increase accountability, motivation, and clarity for founders in the early stages. 2. Long-Term Company-Based Learning Framework At the beginning of Term 1, I would encourage every student to choose two companies aligned with the two domains they are most interested in. Throughout the program, students would apply all in-class and out-of-class learning to these companies as if they were operators or founders. Professors could review progress at the end of each term to ensure structured reflection and application. This approach would promote ownership, long-term thinking, accountability, and first-principles reasoning rather than fragmented, subject-wise learning. It would also help students build deeper domain expertise and an integrated understanding of how businesses function across verticals. 3. Bi-Monthly Informal Founder and VC Interactions Every two months, I would organize informal sessions with startup founders across different sectors and venture capital professionals, including senior associates and vice presidents, not only partners or principals. Even very early-stage founders with evolving ideas could add significant value in answering the hard questions that a student won't ask to a CXO.

acad_feedback_comment: If I was the head of academics at Masters Union, I would share the following: 1. The first thing is that, since we have different professors coming in every time to teach different courses, they often do not have context as to what has previously been taught. For example, if I talk from the perspective of Term 4 for the e-commerce analytics class, the professor has no idea that there are many courses in the category management category as well as in the marketing practicum. Every time a new professor comes, they start from scratch, as they may have not known what we have done previously. I will explain with another example. In Term 4, we have the course on Science of Selling. Now, that professor is trying to teach the Science of Selling from absolute basics, which is not relevant to us. He is trying to teach us almost B2C sales and very roadside transactional sales, or the sales that are by Jews, business development associate, what we are doing. However, it is not relevant to the kind of jobs that we are looking at. We have sales of much larger ticket sizes, the conversations are much more formal, and they are not conversations, which are shot in Hindi and just trying to get the person to buy the product. The B2B marketing course in Semester 3 was a much more relevant course because it is a course where people are taught how to sell and market to B2B enterprises, which is more relevant for us. It is a course where we can see sales, which none of us are actually going to do, and we have learned much more and much more realistically in the B2B marketing course and Science of Selling course for the level we are at. Often times these professors teach multiple courses at Masters Union, like teaching the Master Camp program, teaching the PGP Rise program, teaching the undergraduate program. They do not maybe know the differences in the quality of students that are there in every single program. For example, a professor who teaches at Master Camp before might get confused or might not know that the quality of the student is significantly better at PGP TBM, and they should not benchmark according to that. So, for the visiting faculty, they should be told that the PGP-TBM program has the best students at MU, and they don't have to teach things that they would teach at a Master Camp program.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: I did not take the content creation challenge.
outclass_feedback_comment: The Outclass program is not taken seriously by anyone in the entire batch at MU, barring 5 or 6 students, or maybe 10 students. 90% of the batch is not interested in it. They are just being forced to do it because it is part of the curriculum. I think there is a fundamental gap in the thought process of Masters Union and the thought process of the students coming in. When they are not interested in Outclass, they treat it as an assignment. Why even waste time on it? I understand that the nature of the institution is hands-on learning, but it is not working through the Outclass program. See the presentations. Everybody has just made presentations on random things using AI and has treated it just like an assignment, so why even waste time on it? Learning by doing can be achieved by things like a one-day challenge when we are actually working on real industry problems, and the founders of various companies or senior management are coming and we are presenting solutions through them. Isn't that also learning by doing? Why not replace the Outclass program for 90% of the batches not interested in it by bringing in more one-day challenge opportunities that are as good as hands-on learning as the Outclass, and everybody likes to take it more seriously than the Outclass program? It is an immense waste of time when everybody knows, and it is no secret, that students are not necessarily coming in to build startups. 90% of the students are here to get a job, and everybody is treating it as an assignment as the students are looking for a job. Then why even waste time on it?
careerprep_confidence_comment: The sales practicum from the Career Prep team was not very useful, as the mentor, who is our own senior, was brought in for 3-4 sessions and then the sales practicum ended. The mentor is not available anywhere to reach out to, unlike the other practicums, like the marketing practicum or other subjects, where the mentors are deeply involved with the students in helping them achieve their goals and helping them learn better. The sales practicum was basically a job where the mentor was in a rush to complete the program; she even exited the WhatsApp group within a week of her being added, from when the sales practicum was started. Why bring in such a mentor who's not interested in the student's holistic development with respect to that particular practicum and who just wants to take 4 or 5 sessions, because the Career Prep team has made them come forcefully for the sessions? The mentor is not available, neither on email nor on mentor union. Then why even waste time in bringing such mentors when some much better mentors can be brought in? Why was a mentor who only knows about B2B SaaS sales brought in? Is sales and masters union just B2B SaaS? We have alienated everyone else who's not interested in B2B SaaS by bringing such a mentor who comes into the class and claims that I do not know other aspects other than B2B SaaS and I'll be only focusing on B2B SaaS. Even otherwise, the sales practicum was merely a joke, with one or two simple exercises being done, which were not really relevant to real interview scenarios. In addition to this, the new individual who is brought in for resume help, after nearly half the program the program being completed and not having anybody for resume help, then, when the person is brought in, she asks for your resume, runs it through ChatGPT according to the domain that you have, and then gives you random keywords. She does not have any domain understanding of the domain in reality. She does not even know that there is a difference between product management and other domains. The mentor is not even clear about the domains that MU offers. If you go to her for help, she will take your CV, ask you to mail it to her, and she runs it through ChatGPT and then gives you keywords to write. Completely oblivious that some of those keywords may already be there, she just picks random keywords from a keyword dump that she has and suggests that you write these keywords in your resume. She'll ask you to change random action words at the start of your bullet points, but they make no sense because you're already using action words that are relevant to your resume. If you bring in an individual who's not a proper professional within this domain, who does not understand the kind of job opportunities that MU has, then why is it that we're wasting our time just for a namesake individual who does not understand either the depth of the domain that we have and just needs an AI software to run our resumes so we can do that ourselves? I have serious doubts on the competence of the individual.

program_fix_comment: I want to speak from personal experience as someone who is genuinely serious about building a meaningful career and creating real change both in my own life and for Masters’ Union. I’m someone who works late into the night, constantly pushing myself because I believe I can build something impactful. But I’ve learned that effort alone isn’t enough; ambition needs direction, mentorship, and a sense of support to fully translate into outcomes. I’ve felt that the interaction between Pratham and Cohort 6 has been limited compared to what earlier cohorts experienced. I completely understand the scale of his commitments and responsibilities. Still, I believe there needs to be a middle ground where students like me who are deeply invested in growth can access guidance and meaningful conversations. That connection matters more than it seems. Let me share a simple example from my own headspace: when I think about starting something big, there’s always fear what if the startup fails? What if it doesn’t work? That hesitation is real. But imagine hearing, “Don’t worry, I’ve got your back.” When someone you respect says that, it changes everything. That’s when you take the leap. That’s when you dare to climb higher. Sometimes students don’t need solutions they need reassurance that someone believes in their potential. In a batch of 310 students, I’m sure there are a few people who genuinely want to build something extraordinary. I consider myself one of them. But when interaction is limited, many ambitions stay unspoken. Talent exists, hunger exists, but without a mentor figure pushing, challenging, and encouraging us, that energy can fade or get misdirected. Masters’ Union hasn’t yet had a defining breakout startup, but I strongly believe the potential exists within this cohort. There may already be powerful ideas sitting quietly with students who just need a nudge, validation, or guidance. A small amount of involvement from leadership can unlock disproportionate outcomes. Some suggestions that could help bridge this gap: Regular small-group or open office-hour style interactions with Pratham A mentorship circle for students actively building or ideating startups Late-night or informal founder check-ins where students can speak freely A visible support system that signals: risk-taking is safe here Encouraging leadership to actively identify and nurture high-intent students This isn’t criticism it’s a perspective from someone who wants to do more, build more, and contribute meaningfully. A stronger connection between leadership and students could unlock immense value shaping individual careers while also strengthening Masters’ Union as a place where bold ideas are truly nurtured. I say this not just as feedback, but as someone ready to put in the work if the right guidance exists alongside it. Imagine pratham walking in 2am in the morning and having a conversation with the person working on his or her goal. That’s when perspective would shift. Seeing this 10 folks would burn the midnight oil the next day thinking they would get a chance to interact with pratham if they are really working hard. This is just one things, there’s 100’s of ideas which i have. If u liked the above let’s have a discussion in person for the remaining 99.

program_fix_comment: I’d fix the lack of real personalization in career preparation(includes Academics, Career Prep and Career Outreach). Right now, prep happens in large groups, so it becomes generic. But students are aiming for very different roles, B2B vs D2C product, tech vs management consulting, etc. One-size-fits-all sessions don’t solve individual gaps. If 1:1 for 250 students isn’t practical, create small cohorts of ~10 students with similar goals. For each cohort, mentors, academics, and career services work in a feedback loop to understand industry expectations, identify gaps, and align prep accordingly. Add short, periodic check-ins to keep it real and actionable. Shift from sessions for everyone to gap-solving for small groups. That’s scalable personalization.

May God bless them but I have zero respect for individuals who have ZERO EMPATHY. 2) Category Management course delivered by Sahil Puri. We bid SO HIGH for this course, which turned out to be an absolute disaster. Some feedback basis the 10 classes we had for this course: > We had no idea about the exact evaluation criteria of this course until the last week!! I personally asked Sahil and the TA multiple, multiple times. Sahil would typically tell me, "Bata dunga, kya tension hai". But that "bata dunga" happened in the last week of the course. Imagine that! > No clarity about the exact date or structure or pattern of the End-Term exam until 2-3 days before the End-Term exam. If this isn't the epitome of poor planning, poor communication, and frankly incompetence, I do not know what is...! We chased Sahil and the TA for any information. We were just shunned away. We were almost begging towards the end, such was our helplessness...I don't blame the TA here - he can only share what he knows. But this was frankly shocking. It's unacceptable to mismanage to this degree! > In a 10 lectures course, there were "simulation" exercises which happened in 4-5 lectures. These so called simulations were extremely poorly planned and structured. The class had become a "fish-market" during these supposed simulations - such was the chaos that has repeatedly ensued. Please check lecture videos, you will be amazed that an MBA class at an institute like Masters' Union can be "run" like this. Also, during these simulations (!!), people would frequently step out of the classrooms - some to escape the chaos and the sheer madness, some to just chill, and some in shock thinking, "what a waste of a course". > Extremely biased interactions: There were many instances where it felt like the class was being delivered by Sahil to only two people - these two individuals had prior experience in e-commerce/ quick commerce companies. I don't blame the two individuals here. If they are being asked questions, they will answer. Or if they are sharing their experiences at these e-commerce/ quick commerce companies, that's also fine...However, what is not fine is if it starts feeling like (from time-to-time) a class for these 2 individuals only. 3) Management Strategy grades (a Term 2 subject) were just released yesterday, i.e. 10th Feb. We are in Term 4 and our Term 2 GPA is still not out. The hold-up was due to the Management Strategy course, whose grades were released yesterday. I really respect Dr. Vipin and would love be a part of his courses in the future too, but the fact that the entire Term 2 GPA has been held up till now (we are in Term 4) for one subject is just wrong. As Term 4 MBA students, if we only have Term 1's GPA to add in our Resume, something is clearly wrong with the planning, right?! 4) VIP Outclass Program: Building on the details mentioned under point 3...God knows when the Term 3 GPA will be out...because despite being in Term 4, we are still in the middle of the Term 3 phase of the VIP Outclass program. Term 4 started in 1st week of February, and Term 3 VIP Outclass module ends on 4th March!! This is just wrong!! It's such a disadvantage when results are so massively delayed and we don't have our Term GPA's to add in our Resume. I just cannot fathom that we are in Term 4 with respect to Inclass courses, and in Term 3 with respect to the VIP Outclass module. We are technically in two terms at the same point in time. Just shocking planning. Very disappointed with the Outclass scheduling. It will massively delay our Term 3 Grades and put us all at a disadvantage in the job market - because we won't have our Term 3 grades to add in our Resume till i don't know how long. studentaffairs_value_comment: While building my MVP, I used to work on my startup before coming to MU. program_fix_comment: Before coming to Masters Union, I personally got impressed with the curriculum, which is blended with experiential learning. When I came here during our orientation, Pratham discussed his views on upcoming technology that can be the future, where he talked about gene technology. Then I thought this college was the best decision and that I came here to build something great. Days passed, and when we started working on startup ideas, I pivoted two ideas. Currently, I am working on a B2B startup. Now, the PoD of Masters Union is the VIP programme, and that is what I want to fix. The process behind the VIP is broken, where I think students are being judged on the basis of bias but not on the actual idea. Pratham has enough knowledge on how B2B startups work, but that is not being reflected the same in the Outclass team. If I were sitting in Pratham's chair, the first thing I would do is work with students who are serious about working on their startups rather than people who are doing it just for assignment purposes. I would hire a person who actually has enough knowledge on B2B startups, where he can guide the entire path. Sitting in Pratham's chair, I would love to open three days of my calendar to actually discuss with the students who are working on their startups, which keeps them motivated. Outclass and startups are the vision Masters Union started with, and that team is not automated. Surely, it needs more involvement from Pratham to actually streamline it.

program_fix_comment: If I were sitting in Pratham’s chair, the one thing I would fix immediately is allowing students to specialize earlier in their chosen career path -Startup or Placements - after Term 3. Currently, students are expected to actively engage in both tracks. While this ensures exposure, it also creates cognitive overload and divided focus during a very critical period. What I would do: Introduce a formal opt-in specialization after Term 3. Allow students to choose either the Startup Track or Placement Track. Make non-relevant activities optional rather than mandatory. Provide deeper, structured support tailored to the chosen path (mentors, workshops, evaluations, resources). Why this matters: Focused effort drives better outcomes. By reducing unnecessary juggling, students can channel their time and energy into building stronger startups or securing better job roles - instead of managing parallel expectations. This would improve both student performance and overall institutional outcomes.

By the end of the course we could actually build a model and yeah he's pretty cool too.
acad_feedback_comment: I would like to request more thoughtful and practical scheduling, especially considering the significant time investment that goes into timetable planning. I am sharing this perspective as someone who is also part of the academics team and understands the operational constraints involved. At present, the schedule structure does not feel well-balanced. When concerns are raised, the typical response is that students are expected to manage it, but this approach may not be fully aligned with the realities of our college environment. Our commitments extend beyond academics — we are simultaneously involved in placement preparation and multiple out-of-class activities that require substantial time and consistent effort. There are also specific instances where subject scheduling gaps reduce learning effectiveness. For example, our section had four Economics classes clustered together, followed by a gap of about a month before the next session. Similarly, in Market Research, there was one class and then another only after about a month. In practice, especially during peak placement season, it is not realistic to expect students to retain continuity or come fully revised after such long gaps, given the volume of parallel responsibilities. Although the total number of classes may have been reduced, the current distribution pattern does not create meaningful, usable time blocks for revision, preparation, and other essential work. A more consolidated and continuity-focused scheduling approach would significantly improve both learning outcomes and students’ ability to manage academic and non-academic priorities effectively.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: Did not opt for this challenge
outclass_feedback_comment: My consolidated feedback for the Outclass program is that the current structure feels overly academic and assignment-driven for something that is meant to support real startup building. It may be useful to reassess whether mandatory, theory-heavy assignments are the right approach for student startups. Many assignments did not translate into meaningful startup progress and often required more time than actual venture work. The high frequency of submissions and rigid timelines made the process feel compliance-focused rather than outcome-focused. Activities such as interviews and validation tasks were executed largely for submission purposes, which reduced the depth and quality of insights generated. A mentor-led, discussion-based model with weekly progress review slots would likely be more effective than multiple structured assignments. Regular feedback conversations would provide more practical guidance and flexibility. From an evaluation standpoint, there is also a lack of transparency. Despite strong performance in pre-seed value assignments, our startup was not selected for the demo day pitch, and no specific feedback was shared on where we fell short. Seeing similar startups get selected without understanding the differentiation criteria makes it difficult to identify gaps and improve. A clearer feedback and review mechanism after evaluations would significantly enhance learning and alignment.
careerprep_confidence_comment: The Career Prep team has been consistently prompt in sharing LMP details and facilitating mentor connections. The communication and coordination from their side have been timely and reliable. I also appreciate that whenever I am in the right headspace to seek guidance or support, the team is approachable and responsive. Overall, the support system feels accessible and student-friendly.
careerservices_feedback_comment: My feedback for the Outreach/Placement team is that most updates are currently shared only when students actively follow up. Given that students are simultaneously managing academics, startup work, and high-volume job applications each day, it becomes difficult to also continuously track placement POCs and application statuses independently. It would be more effective if updates regarding company outreach, drive status, or changes in timelines are shared proactively rather than only on request. Regular, structured communication would reduce uncertainty and help students plan their efforts better. Additionally, the number of placement drives being conducted appears lower than what was communicated during the orientation phase. The positioning and expectations set earlier regarding institutional reach and company participation do not seem fully reflected in the current volume of campus opportunities. Greater transparency on outreach progress and company pipeline visibility would be helpful for expectation alignment.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: 1. Pranav Dedhia - Exceptional subject knowledge, quite patient, less on dramatics, comes to class teaches and no drama.
studentaffairs_value_comment: Nothing specific, quite unclear on the question
program_nps_reason_comment: Overall, my feedback is that the program has not fully met expectations across its three core pillars — academics, entrepreneurship support, and placements. The value delivery across these areas has felt limited relative to what was communicated at the outset. On the academic side, much of the effective learning appears to depend on self-study. From an entrepreneurship perspective, the support structure has not consistently translated into practical, outcome-driven guidance. On the placements front, students are largely relying on their own efforts for applications and opportunities. At present, it feels as though success across academics, startup development, and career outcomes depends primarily on individual effort rather than structured institutional support. Strengthening delivery, accountability, and hands-on guidance across these three pillars would significantly improve the overall student experience.
program_positive_comment: I appreciate the initiative of providing fruits in the morning — it is a thoughtful and healthy addition, and I would strongly recommend that it be continued. It may also help to increase the quantity or extend the availability window, as students with later morning classes (such as 11 a.m.) often miss out. Additionally, some of the elective courses were delivered by very effective professors and created strong learning value. It would be beneficial to continue engaging those faculty members for future cohorts and electives.
program_fix_comment: My key leadership-related feedback is the importance of consistently showing up and staying engaged with the batch, even when it is not formally mandated. Regular on-ground presence and direct interaction with the cohort would help leadership better understand real student challenges and program gaps. The two immediate improvements I would suggest are as follows. First, conduct a structured mid-stage review of all startups currently being built, including an audit of the feedback provided by the Outclass team and an additional independent review at the leadership level. The objective should be to ensure students receive practical, candid, and actionable feedback early enough to pivot and strengthen their startups before major evaluation milestones. Second, review academic delivery alongside curriculum design. While many courses and syllabi are strong on paper, it is important to assess whether they are being effectively delivered and absorbed. This should include evaluating both teaching quality and scheduling patterns, to ensure that course structure and timing actually support learning retention rather than limiting it.

program_fix_comment: MU’s Current Situation & Misalignment At Masters' Union, the institutional ethos is clear with "Entrepreneurship-first approach" & "Learn by doing". That vision is inspiring and differentiating. However, there is a practical misalignment. While the brand and philosophy emphasize venture-building, 70–80% of students are not immediately positioned to start their entrepreneurial journey post-PGP. Many need financial stability, industry exposure, domain depth, or simply time before taking entrepreneurial risk. The majority of students depend on strong career outcomes. In this situation, Career Outreach should function as a strategic backbone of the institution. Instead, there are repeated instances of: 1) Fake Job listings being posted without verified recruiter confirmation 2) Students applying and receiving no response 3) No clarity on whether roles are active, closed, or even real 4) Data being shared without verifiable backing If I were in Pratham’s position, the first thing I would immediately fix is the lack of accountability within the Career Outreach team. Right now, the biggest issue is not placements, it is the absence of measurable accountability. Because nothing is measured transparently: 1) There is no incentive to ensure recruiter responsiveness 2) There is no ownership of unresolved student concerns 3) There is no performance pressure to improve quality of listings Students are losing trust in the system. Without accountability metrics, the Career Outreach team operates without clear responsibility for outcomes. How I Would Fix It: Outcome-Based KPIs with Public Transparency 3 mandatory metrics tracked monthly: 1) % of job listings with verified recruiter confirmation (At least 90% of posted listings must have confirmed recruiter validation within the last 14 days) 2) Average recruiter response time (target under 7 days for first-level response) 3) % of applications receiving first-level response (70–80% of applications should receive a first-level response, i.e., interview/rejection/screening acknowledgment) This pushes the team to track application pipelines, Follow up actively, & maintain recruiter relationships Students should not feel like their applications disappear into a black hole. If we define measurable, outcome-based KPIs and publish them transparently every month, the Career Outreach team at Masters’ Union will transform from a reactive department to a performance-driven one.

He explained concepts in a very simple and clear way, and what I appreciated most was that he never moved ahead if even one student was stuck. For someone like me who did not have a strong background in financial modeling, this made a huge difference. His classes were practical, engaging, and extremely helpful. Even the assignments he gave were thoughtfully designed and helped me apply what I learned. Sharavn Tickoo’s Product Analytics sessions were equally impactful. He broke down complex analytics concepts into very simple, easy to understand language. This made the subject much more approachable and helped me build confidence in understanding and applying analytics in real scenarios.
acad_feedback_comment: I would like to share one suggestion regarding the scheduling of sessions. In this term for Macroeconomics, the first three sessions were conducted in the second or third week of December, while the remaining two sessions were held much later in the third and fourth week of January. This long gap disrupted the continuity of learning. It broke the flow of the subject and made it harder to stay connected with the concepts that were taught earlier. Since this subject had only five sessions and we have six working days in a week, it could have been scheduled within a single week and completed without long interruptions. I understand that this may not always be possible with visiting faculty members due to their availability, but this approach might be more feasible for in house faculty. I believe that minimizing such gaps between sessions would help students maintain focus and get a better learning experience. I would also like to add one more suggestion regarding submissions and deadlines. If a term ends on a specific date, for example 28 January, then all submissions related to that term should also be completed by the same date. This includes projects, one day challenges, macro projects, and even small assignments. When submissions get pushed into the next term, it becomes difficult to focus on the new subjects because students are still occupied with pending work from the previous term. While this may be done to manage workload, it often has the opposite effect and creates unnecessary overlap and stress. I believe it would be more effective if all academic requirements of a term are completed within that term itself, so that students can start the next term with a clear mind and better focus.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: I had initially opted for it, but later withdrew to better manage my workload.
outclass_feedback_comment: Overall, I am quite satisfied with how the Outclass sessions are being conducted. The structure and quality of the sessions have been really good. I have one small suggestion regarding scheduling. In both Term 2 and Term 3, some sessions were scheduled at very short notice. For example, the first MVP demo session this term was scheduled around 36 to 48 hours in advance. It would be very helpful if such sessions could be scheduled at least a week in advance. I usually plan my entire week on Sundays, allocating time for classes, mentor calls, and thesis work. When a session is scheduled at short notice, it disrupts this plan and creates difficulty in managing other commitments. After the first session, the schedule was more aligned, which was great to see. Going forward, having the schedule communicated about a week earlier would make it much easier to plan and manage time effectively.
careerprep_confidence_comment: Yes, Career Prep has definitely made me more confident in preparing for placements. I am someone who regularly develops new ideas and creates basic outlines before discussing them with the Career Prep team. Whenever I approach them with these ideas, they provide very constructive feedback and help me refine my thinking. They also ensure proper follow up whenever something needs improvement or gets stuck, which has been very helpful for me. I often work with tight timelines and sometimes request meetings at short notice. Even then, they make the effort to accommodate me and provide guidance whenever possible. I am overall very satisfied with the support from Career Prep, and I would especially like to mention Vidhu Goel for being extremely helpful throughout this process.
careerprep_feedback_comment: While I am satisfied with most aspects of Career Prep, I would like to share some feedback regarding the A and M touchpoint sessions. My A and M touchpoint took place in Term 3. During the session, I was asked to describe my last six months with a strong opening hook. I assumed this exercise was meant to help with interview preparation. However, I was later told that it was not intended for interviews and was simply an exercise to speak out loud. This left me a bit unclear about the purpose and practical value of the session. I feel that these touchpoints could be made more impactful if they are more closely aligned with upcoming placement opportunities. For example, Career Services usually knows which companies are scheduled to visit in the coming weeks. If this information is shared with the Career Prep team, A and M mentors could connect with students who are particularly interested in those companies and guide them in a more targeted way. This could include suggestions on how to tailor resumes, how to position their experiences, or even how to prepare specific ideas or presentations that align with the company’s business. Such alignment would make the touchpoints more practical and directly useful for placements.
careerservices_feedback_comment: I would like to share feedback in two areas regarding Career Services and Outreach. First, regarding the companies coming to campus. Many of the roles seem to be lateral in nature, especially for positions like Founder’s Office or Strategy, which I understand are often lateral hires in most companies. However, in several cases where I applied to roles that closely match my past experience and domain, I have not received any clarity on the outcome of my applications. While there is a tracking sheet and I can see that it is being updated, it still does not clearly communicate what has happened to many applications. For example, I applied to around ten to twelve companies. I received rejection updates from two of them, but for the remaining applications, there is no clear status. It is unclear whether the application was rejected, not considered, or if the position was closed. In some cases, I can still see the same role being posted publicly, which adds to the confusion. Having more transparent and timely updates on application status would be very helpful. Second, regarding outreach support. Based on the thesis and sector decks I prepared, I was advised to share a list of target companies and was told that the team would help in reaching out. I shared this list some time ago. Since then, I have been following up regularly, but I have not received any concrete update or direction on the next steps. At this point, I feel I have done the necessary groundwork from my side in terms of preparation and clarity on the roles and companies I am targeting. Support from the Career Services team in initiating and managing outreach to these companies would make a significant difference.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: Not Attended any CXO Session this time
masterrelations_feedback_comment: I would like to share a few suggestions regarding the Master Relations and CXO sessions. First, while it is great to hear about the journeys and experiences of the CXOs who visit, many sessions tend to focus more on their personal stories rather than deeper industry insights. As students, we are very keen to understand current industry trends, challenges, and practical perspectives that can help us prepare better for specific sectors. Second, since these CXO interactions help build a certain level of trust and familiarity, this could be an opportunity to explore more tangible outcomes such as live projects or internship opportunities for students. This is an area where closer coordination between the Master Relations team and Career Services could create meaningful value. Third, these sessions could be made more targeted. For example, if a healthcare startup CXO is visiting, students who are particularly interested in the healthcare sector could be informed in advance and encouraged to prepare a short deck, insights, or questions. A small group interaction of fifteen to twenty minutes with prepared students could make the discussion much more practical and impactful for both sides.
studentaffairs_value_comment: In Term 3, the events and activities that were organized added good value to the overall student experience. At the same time, I was primarily focused on preparing for placements during this term, so I was not deeply involved in many student activities. Because of this, I may not have experienced this aspect as extensively, but the events that did take place were well organized and enjoyable.
studentaffairs_feedback_comment: I would like to share a suggestion for future batches regarding the scheduling of major student events such as Unmute. I was very keen on attending Unmute this time, but due to placement related commitments, I could not participate. I also know a few of my friends who felt the same. Hosting such a cultural event in February feels a bit late, as many students are either preparing for interviews, involved in internships, or focused on placement activities during this period. It might be more suitable to schedule Unmute in November or December, when students are relatively more free and can participate with greater enthusiasm. Similarly, while cultural celebrations tied to specific festivals will naturally happen on their respective dates, sports activities and other events could be planned earlier in the academic calendar and completed in advance. This period is usually when students begin focusing seriously on placements, so earlier scheduling may lead to better participation and engagement.
tech_careercoach_comment: I would like to share a few suggestions to improve the Career Coach platform. First, a live application tracking feature within the platform itself would be very helpful, instead of relying on a separate tracking sheet. This would make it easier to understand the real time status of applications in one place. Second, the job postings on the platform can be improved. At times, company websites or detailed job descriptions are either missing or not presented clearly. Ensuring that descriptions are complete and properly formatted would make it easier to read and understand the opportunity. Third, when trying to upload updated resumes, the platform often shows errors or the updated file does not reflect properly. Making this process smoother would be very helpful. Apart from these points, the platform is functioning well overall.
tech_mentorunion_comment: I understand that MentorUnion is a relatively new platform developed internally, and overall it is a good initiative. I would like to share a few practical suggestions based on my experience. First, rescheduling sessions is often difficult from the student side. Many times, I have to request the mentor to cancel or modify the session instead of being able to do it myself through the platform. Making rescheduling easier for students would improve the experience. Second, when taking repeated sessions with the same mentor, the platform still requires filling out the same initial form every time. Since the mentor is already familiar with the context and purpose of the sessions, this feels repetitive and unnecessary. This was handled more smoothly in the earlier system, where repeated calls did not require re entering the same information. Third, students currently do not have the option to record MentorUnion calls. Mentors often share very valuable insights that are difficult to note down during the conversation. If recording is enabled with proper consent, such as requiring mentor approval before recording starts, it would be extremely helpful while also respecting privacy. Apart from these points, the overall concept and experience of MentorUnion is positive.
tech_lms_comment: I would like to highlight a concern regarding the reliability of LMS. Quite often, the platform becomes unavailable or very slow, especially when many students try to access it at the same time. A recent example was the online Managerial Accounting quiz, where around 120 students attempted to log in at once. As soon as everyone accessed the platform, it went down. This issue also occurs when faculty upload important materials or problem statements and the entire class tries to access them simultaneously. The platform struggles to handle the load, which creates inconvenience and stress during important academic activities. Improving the platform’s stability and its ability to handle higher traffic at peak times would greatly improve the overall experience for students. Additionally, it would be helpful to have deadline reminders. For example, if an assignment deadline is at midnight, receiving a notification two or three hours in advance would be very useful. While students are expected to manage deadlines, multiple commitments can sometimes lead to unintentional misses, and such reminders could help prevent that.
program_nps_reason_comment: The biggest reason for my score is that this term was very good in terms of the quality of lectures and overall learning experience. My main suggestions relate to scheduling and timelines. In a few subjects, some sessions were conducted at the beginning of the month and the remaining sessions were held much later. This broke the flow of learning and made it harder to stay connected with the subject. I also feel that if a term ends on a specific date, all assignments, ODCs, and projects related to that term should be completed by then, instead of being carried forward into the next term. Additionally, there have been instances where assignment scores were not uploaded on time. Since many of these evaluations are done through forms or simple submissions, timely updates of scores should be manageable and would help students track their performance better.
program_positive_comment: What worked best for me this term was the balanced academic schedule, which gave me enough time to focus on multiple areas without feeling rushed. I had good opportunities to connect with mentors, spend quality time on case preparation, and work properly on assignments. The spacing of academic activities allowed me to manage my time effectively, and this is something that should definitely be continued.
program_fix_comment: If I were in Pratham’s position, the first thing I would fix is the lack of clear visibility around placements. Right now, students do not have a clear idea of which companies are expected to come and when. There are many lateral movements happening, but apart from a shared tracker, there is limited clarity. Given that this is a 16 month rigorous program and placements are naturally challenging, what students really need alongside the effort is clear communication and visibility. By visibility, I do not mean only town halls or general updates. I mean a simple and transparent schedule that shows which companies are expected in the coming weeks. This would help students prepare in a more focused way and also build patience and confidence that opportunities are in the pipeline. To implement this, I would ensure better coordination between Career Services and the teams managing CXO and industry interactions. As suggested earlier, CXO sessions can be used more strategically to bring in live projects, problem statements, or closer engagement with companies. This approach can partially address the visibility issue while also creating more practical engagement opportunities for students.

acad_feedback_comment: I’d like to share some honest and constructive feedback, which I’ve been reflecting on since Term 1 and felt more strongly by Term 3. My primary domain is Marketing, and in today’s ecosystem—especially with the rise of e-commerce and quick commerce—Performance Marketing and Growth roles are among the most in-demand and highest-paying career paths. However, not all students come in with prior exposure to performance marketing. Given the course structure, particularly in Dropshipping, performance marketing felt like a core foundational skill that we were expected to already know. In practice, performance marketing is not just about running ads—it is about optimisation, experimentation, reading data, and improving performance over time, which typically comes either through hands-on experience or expert-led teaching. I believe that if performance marketing fundamentals and ad optimisation had been introduced more deeply during Term 1 Outclass, it would have significantly strengthened our skill base. By Term 3, students could then have naturally progressed to more advanced concepts like growth engines and scalable growth models. Instead, for many of us, Term 3 felt overwhelming because we were directly introduced to growth frameworks without having a solid grounding in how growth is actually driven at the execution level. Since performance marketing is a core skill in Dropshipping, it also gives students a strong advantage in VIP, making this gap more noticeable over time. On a personal note, I was fortunate to have a teammate during Term 1 who shared some knowledge around performance marketing, which helped me keep up. However, many students did not have this exposure, making it harder for them to fully grasp the later concepts. I’m sharing this purely as constructive feedback, with the intent of strengthening the learning experience for future cohorts. This is not to point fingers at any individual or team, but to highlight a potential area where the curriculum could be made even more impactful.

program_nps_reason_comment: Initially, I had very different expectations when I joined Masters’ Union. I believed the experience would be rigorous, profile-driven, and focused on real learning. However, after joining, I started to regret the decision and found myself just waiting for this phase to end. Repeatedly hearing comments about how ‘this cohort is like this’ only reinforced that feeling—and honestly, I agree there are gaps. But the real question is: whose responsibility is this? It seems the admissions process focuses largely on English-speaking ability and surface-level impressions, rather than truly understanding a candidate’s background, experience, and depth of knowledge. Students are selected, but their profiles are not genuinely evaluated. When I joined MU, the admissions team even asked me for feedback without knowing that I am an Everest summiteer. If such a significant part of my profile was missed, it makes me question how my candidature was assessed in the first place. From that very moment, my interest in participating or engaging in activities here began to decline.
program_positive_comment: First, train the team properly; only then recruit a strong cohort. The academic quality of the term will improve automatically.
program_fix_comment: If I were sitting on Pratham’s chair, the first thing I would fix is the admissions and engagement quality—starting with the people who represent Masters’ Union. Masters’ Union has positioned itself extremely well as a modern, outcome-driven institution. However, if it truly wants to sustain itself in the long run and compete at a global level, it cannot afford to recruit students based only on surface-level indicators like confidence, English fluency, or presentation skills. The focus must shift toward understanding real profiles—experience, depth of knowledge, mindset, and learning intent. At the same time, internal teams must be trained to engage deeply and consistently with students. I am not pointing fingers at the entire team, but even one disengaged or unprepared member can significantly impact the MU brand and student experience. I would implement stricter, profile-driven admission evaluations and structured training for internal teams to ensure alignment with MU’s values. If the right people are selected and supported from the start, the cohort quality and academic experience will automatically improve.

program_fix_comment: If I were in Pratham Mittal’s chair, I would institutionalize mental and physical energy systems to prevent burnout and improve long-term student performance. MBA programs are high-intensity environments, but students are rarely taught how to manage energy, stress, and focus sustainably. I’d introduce performance coaching sessions to help students prioritize and align their goals, energy management workshops covering sleep, deep work, and cognitive optimization, honest founder talks about stress and resilience, and structured guilt-free off-days to normalize recovery. The idea isn’t to reduce ambition, but to channel it sustainably — because peak performance is only possible when it’s sustainable.
