acad_most_impact_comment: Rajat Mathur sir - Loved leadership lab to the fullest. Bhupesh sir & Garima ma'am - As always, they truly stood out. The Market Research course, in particular, gave me a new level of confidence, it genuinely made me feel capable of aiming for a 25–30 LPA opportunity with conviction. Garima Ma’am’s course, on the other hand, provided rich context around global economic developments. Her ability to simplify macroeconomics and make it intuitive made what initially felt complex seem surprisingly manageable almost like a cakewalk. Pranav Dhedia - As always, they truly stood out. The Market Research course, in particular, gave me a new level of confidence, it genuinely made me feel capable of aiming for a 25–30 LPA opportunity with conviction.
acad_feedback_comment: 1. Leadership Lab – I truly enjoyed Rajat Mathur sir’s Leadership Lab 3 course. The sessions were insightful and engaging. However, I noticed that many students did not attend consistently across all four sessions. During one of our section classes, I learned from Rajat sir that the course was an elective. Personally, I believe it should continue to remain an elective for future cohorts as well. When someone of Rajat Mathur sir’s stature is teaching, the classroom should ideally consist of students who are genuinely interested and committed enough to attend diligently and participate actively. That would preserve the seriousness and quality of engagement the course deserves. 2. Strategic Leadership – While Manu sir’s sessions were good overall, I feel they could have been even stronger with clearer session structuring. A recurring issue was that many students did not complete the assigned case studies beforehand. In such situations, there should ideally be some mechanism to ensure pre-reads are done, so that discussions can be deeper and more meaningful. I’ve observed that Vipin sir follows a very defined structure while conducting case discussions. I’m not suggesting Manu sir replicate that exact format, but having a more consistent and structured approach to case analysis would have made the sessions smoother and more engaging. That said, I genuinely enjoyed Manu sir’s classes. The content and the choice of case studies were excellent. The Saffronart case, in particular, still comes up in discussions among my friends and me which speaks to its impact. 3. Managerial Accounting – Course was good overall. However, across the five sessions, I occasionally felt Nimisha ma’am seemed slightly low on confidence. Additionally, we spent nearly three classes on CM1 calculations, and I believe that time distribution could have been optimized by allocating relatively more focus to budgeting concepts. A slight restructuring of content balance could make the course even stronger. 4. Corporate Strategy & Strategic Leadership – I thoroughly enjoyed Vipin sir’s courses this term. After completing Corporate Strategy and Strategic Leadership, I strongly resonate with the Dunning-Kruger graph he showed in the last session of Management Strategy in Term 2. He truly is a gem in the form of a professor for Masters’ Union. His structured thinking, clarity, and rigor consistently elevate the learning experience. 5. Financial Modelling & Macro Economics - I loved Pranav sir’s Financial Modelling course. Coming from an engineering background, financial modelling initially seemed intimidating, but his teaching style made the learning process engaging and accessible. It was also wonderful to have Garima ma’am back. Her teaching brings clarity and depth to complex topics. I would strongly encourage floating more courses by Garima ma’am and Nandini ma’am for YLC in upcoming terms.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: The Content Creation Challenge helped me in meaningful ways. More importantly, it has slowly helped me overcome certain inhibitions and become more confident in public. In many ways, it feels like I’m revisiting a dream I had back in 2016 of becoming a YouTube vlogger except now, in 2026, I’m expressing that through mini vlogs and moments from my life on Instagram. That personal growth has been valuable. However, I would like to share an honest perspective. The progress I experienced did not necessarily stem from the sessions conducted by the Outclass team. It primarily came from my intention to remain consistent and complete the challenge assignments and to be candid, grades were the only motivating factor to do it diligently. I do acknowledge that clarity about niche and voice comes from experimentation. But content creation from ideation to scripting, shooting, editing, and posting requires significant time and mental bandwidth. I was able to commit to the process diligently for the first three weeks. However, once the end-term week kicked in, it became extremely difficult to sustain that level of effort. Additionally, for someone who is trying to pursue VIP seriously, balancing both initiatives becomes challenging unless the content creation challenge is directly aligned with the same focus area, it's very difficult to pursue both simultaneously.
outclass_feedback_comment: I truly appreciated the moment during the VIP Pre-Seed orientation when Saksham walked us through the numbers and demonstrated how building and running a business can generate comparable if not greater wealth than a 30-year consulting career. Please don’t tone down that level of enthusiasm, Saksham. That energy is powerful and inspiring. It genuinely motivated me to take the idea of building something of my own much more seriously. At the same time, I’ve been reflecting on the sequencing of certain programs. Personally, I found the structure followed by earlier cohorts completing the Content Creation Challenge in Term 2 and beginning VIP in Term 3 to make a lot of sense. The alignment between in-class and out-class learning feels particularly important. For instance, while we are currently learning Market Research in class, we are applying similar principles to our MVP decks. I can clearly see how much more structured, data-backed, and coherent my current MVP deck is compared to my earlier pre-seed deck. A significant part of that improvement stems from the clarity and rigor developed through the Market Research course. That said, I genuinely believe that if Market Research had been paired with the pre-seed phase with MR in-class and pre-seed implementation in out-class I would have felt equally confident, if not even more so, during the initial stages of building. The synchronization between theory and immediate application could have amplified both confidence and execution quality.
careerprep_confidence_comment: The Prep Team has been genuinely helpful when it comes to interview preparation. I’ve seen firsthand how my friends received consistent guidance and support at every stage of their interview journeys. I also reached out to Vidhu and Radhika for guidance while applying to a role at my dream company. They took the time to understand my situation, hear me out patiently, and offer thoughtful advice and direction. I appreciate their support. Additionally, I really value the introduction of the IRM sessions.
careerservices_feedback_comment: Since internship and job opportunities began circulating, I was hoping to see at least a few companies from the “dream companies” sheet that we had filled out back in August or September. Given the timeline, I believe there was sufficient opportunity to initiate outreach efforts toward some of those organizations, so that students could at least get a fair shot at interviewing with companies they genuinely aspire to join. Unfortunately, that didn’t seem to materialize. That said, I would like to give a shoutout to Simran, Siddhartha, and Dibyendu sir. They have been doing commendable work. From what my friends have shared, Dibyendu sir, in particular, has been extremely supportive throughout their application and interview processes. His guidance and involvement have clearly made a positive difference in their preparation and confidence.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: Nitin Gadkari's session and Anupam Mittal's session. Both were amazing. I watched Kishore Biyani's session on YT as I couldn't attend that session when it was happening live. But the format of that CXO session was amazing where we were actually going through the building a business process together.
masterrelations_feedback_comment: I would strongly request that we explore bringing in leaders from the companies listed in our Dream Companies sheet. I believe this would serve multiple purposes beyond placements. It would create meaningful networking opportunities with CXOs and senior leaders not just for referrals, but for gaining deeper insights into the industry, understanding specific roles, and learning about company culture firsthand. That kind of exposure can be incredibly valuable for long-term career clarity and strategic decision-making. Also, I loved the recent roundtable conversation format with Pallavi, the founder of GoStops. That session felt far more engaging and impactful compared to traditional large-audience CXO talks, which often become one-way interactions with limited opportunity for real engagement. I would strongly encourage more sessions in the roundtable format smaller, interactive discussions that allow for genuine dialogue and relationship-building. In fact, if the guest speaker is from a company featured in students’ Dream Companies list, such intimate sessions would be even more powerful. They would provide students with a rare opportunity to meaningfully interact with leaders they genuinely aspire to work with, rather than participating in a broader, less personalized open session.
studentaffairs_value_comment: The movie night that happened a few weeks ago was really good. I thoroughly enjoyed on that day. And of course UnMute & Bollywood Night were great.
studentaffairs_feedback_comment: Even though we had shared the FOCOS Club (Ghost Ops) event plan well in advance in December, we were unable to receive the required operational support from the Student Affairs team to conduct the event on campus. As a result, due to limited funds to execute it independently, we shifted the event online. One of the reasons communicated to us later was that the participants were not from top B-schools and that sponsors were not yet confirmed. While I understand the perspective behind these considerations, it would have been extremely helpful if these expectations had been clearly communicated at the initial stage. With earlier clarity, we could have structured the event, outreach strategy, and sponsorship efforts differently. More broadly, I believe that among all teams, the Student Affairs team should ideally be the most structured, robust, and seamlessly accessible to students. To their credit, accessibility largely exists. However, at times, the execution feels slightly chaotic, with instances of miscommunication, gaps in information flow, or delays in response. There are moments when processes do not appear fully standardized, or there isn’t complete clarity on how certain concerns should be escalated or resolved. From my previous professional experience, documentation of even small communications was essential to ensure transparency, accountability, and alignment. I believe that introducing clearer communication documentation and defined processes could significantly reduce ambiguity and create stronger trust on both sides. This perspective is not coming from criticism, but from a genuine desire to see the system operate at its highest potential, especially in an institution that emphasizes professionalism, ownership, and operational excellence.
tech_careercoach_comment: Could please give us an option of uploading our resume when sometimes we want to customise the resume for a particular role? I see the option in Career Coach, but somehow was not able to access it or maybe it was disabled.
tech_lms_comment: Sometimes the resume is not getting updated with a new one. I faced this recently. Although I flagged to the LMS team when they recently met us after MVP townhall. And the team mentioned that they'll be able to fix this in next 2 weeks.
program_nps_reason_comment: Very honestly, the Market Research course along with all of Bhupesh Sir’s courses, combined with the opportunity we have here to build something of our own, has made me incredibly happy. A few days ago, I was reflecting on how I’ve changed over the past eight months. At first, I couldn’t pinpoint anything concrete. But the day I submitted our MR report, it struck me, I’ve become someone who is about 70% closer to the person I always wanted to be: someone who holds strong, well-formed opinions and can back them with data and logic. And for me, that feels like a genuinely big win. That being said, I have also been reflecting on the cohort experience. I’m not sure whether it’s the specific mix of this batch or just circumstantial factors, but I personally haven’t found as many people who are deeply passionate about building, solution-oriented in their thinking, or willing to take unconventional paths without fear of hustling regardless of age or background. As business students, I feel we should naturally gravitate toward creating solutions rather than primarily discussing problems with the system. One of the most valuable aspects of Masters’ Union is the flexibility it offers — the freedom to design our own journey, pursue independent projects, experiment, and build. That autonomy is powerful. However, I sometimes feel that only a small percentage of students are truly leveraging that freedom to its fullest potential. At times, it feels as though some students approach the program with a more traditional B-school mindset similar to how one might approach an IIM with the primary focus being placements as quickly as possible. There is nothing inherently wrong with that goal, but irrespective of the system, I believe personal ambition and proactive effort toward one’s own dreams should remain central. Before joining, I often heard in podcasts that the cohort here is incredibly diverse and driven. While diversity in backgrounds certainly exists, I feel that the overall intensity and quality of ambition could be stronger. My intention in saying this isn’t criticism for the sake of it, but rather a desire to see the ecosystem push everyone including me to operate at an even higher level.
program_positive_comment: I'm most happy with In-class this time because of the courses offered. And also the IRM sessions, they are thoughtful thing to add to our prep. It not just opens wide range of knowledge & exposure to that industry, but also acts as a very good way network with master.
program_fix_comment: 1. Institutionalizing the "Founder Magic" The recent dip in NPS scores highlights a critical inflection point in our growth: the transition from a founder-driven system to a self-sustaining institution. In the early stages, the brand's energy was fueled by the direct involvement and charisma of Pratham himself and a few key faculties. However, to scale effectively, we must build a robust, decentralized systems that delivers a consistent student experience across departments without being dependent on specific individuals. We need to move from "person-led" success to "process-driven" excellence, ensuring that the essence of the school is baked into the system itself, rather than resting on the shoulders of a few. 2. Guarding the Startup DNA As we increase our cohort sizes, we face a growing "cultural drift" within our management teams, I feel. There is a disconnect between the school’s core identity; a new-age, fast-paced, and robust business disruptor and a few dull or not so MU coded hires. We are a startup that is evolving at its core every single day; if our team doesn't share that "hustle" ethos, the system will eventually stagnate. Moving forward, we face a binary choice: we must either aggressively recruit leadership that aligns with our high-velocity startup DNA or intentionally slow our expansion to ensure our cultural foundation doesn't crumble under the weight of rapid growth. 3. Resolving the Marketing Paradox Finally, we must address the growing irony between what we teach in our classrooms and how we market ourselves to the world. Our curriculum champions "the road not taken" and the spirit of building from scratch, yet our current admission team execution are casting too wide a net, attracting students who are still seeking a traditional, "safe" B-school experience. This misalignment of expectations is a primary driver of dissatisfaction; we are attracting "degree-seekers" when we should be targeting "builders." We need to audit our brand image to ensure we are projecting a persona that attracts the gritty, high-hustle candidates we were built for, rather than diluting our brand equity for the sake of volume.

acad_feedback_comment: I believe the academic structure and curriculum are well thought out and aligned with industry expectations. There have been instances where frequent changes or poorly spaced schedules disrupted the regular routine, which made it challenging to maintain consistency and productivity. A more structured and predictable timetable, planned well in advance, would help students manage their time better and improve overall engagement. Better coordination between academic activities and class schedules would further enhance the learning experience.

Even with limited participation, I gained clarity on the key skills required to identify a niche, choose the right platform, and understand different content formats.
outclass_feedback_comment: The Outclass initiatives are well-intentioned and offer exposure beyond the regular academic curriculum. However, I feel there is scope for introducing greater flexibility in how students engage with these activities. Many students simultaneously manage internships, applications, academic commitments, and examinations, which can make mandatory participation challenging at times. Allowing students some choice in selecting Outclass activities similar to how electives are chosen would make the experience more meaningful and aligned with individual goals. For instance, while the Content Creation Challenge may be beneficial for some, it may not be relevant for everyone due to personal interests or professional direction.
careerprep_confidence_comment: Yes, Career Prep has helped me feel more confident in preparing for placements. The team has taken multiple focused initiatives to support students, including structured guidance, preparatory sessions, and continuous engagement, which has been reassuring throughout the process.
careerprep_feedback_comment: Career Prep has been helpful overall, especially in terms of guidance and preparedness for placements. However, one area where the process could be improved is the frequency of resume update submissions. Being asked to upload updated resumes repeatedly within short intervals often creates confusion and an unnecessary administrative burden, especially when there are no significant profile changes within one or two months. A more streamlined approach such as a one-time comprehensive resume submission or updates only when there is a substantial change would be more efficient. This would allow students to focus better on internships, academics, applications, and skill development. Similarly, modules such as behavioral preparation or mock sessions could be conducted in a more consolidated, one-shot format rather than being stretched across the entire process.
careerservices_feedback_comment: I feel the current outreach process could benefit from a more profile-driven and research-oriented approach. At present, internships appear to be floated in a generic manner, without sufficient alignment to students’ academic backgrounds or prior industry experience. In my case, coming from a pharmaceutical background, none of the internship opportunities shared were relevant to my profile or industry. Despite multiple applications and repeated communication with the Career Prep and Career Services teams, there was no visible progress in sourcing roles aligned to my domain. As a result, I had to independently secure an internship on my own. Additionally, the stipend levels offered for many internships do not meet the expectations of an MBA program. While compensation may vary, if stipends are low, it would be far more valuable to collaborate with established or legacy organisations, as this significantly strengthens a student’s profile and improves long-term placement outcomes. Several startups offered lacked clarity in terms of job descriptions, organisational structure, or even a credible digital presence, which made it difficult to assess learning value. A more targeted outreach strategy focused on industry relevance, quality of organisations, and student profiles would greatly enhance the effectiveness and credibility of Career Services.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: I particularly enjoyed the sessions conducted by Rajat Sir and Bhupesh Sir. The way they explained ideas made it easy for me to grasp not just the what, but also the why behind the concepts. Their sessions were highly engaging, and the learning genuinely stayed with me even after the classes. More importantly, the skills and perspectives they focused on are highly relevant to today’s industry requirements. The emphasis on practical application and real-world relevance made their sessions extremely valuable for my overall learning and professional development.
studentaffairs_value_comment: In Term 3, the most valuable part of the student experience was the focus on practical, application-based learning
program_nps_reason_comment: The single biggest reason for my score is the peer learning environment, which has been one of the strongest aspects of my MBA experience. program_positive_comment: What worked best for me this term was the CXO sessions and life-skill–oriented subjects such as the Leadership Lab. program_fix_comment: If I were in Pratham’s position, the first thing I would fix is the Career Services function, with a clear focus on quality, relevance, and standards rather than volume. If the goal is to build a B-school that competes with other leading institutions not just in numbers but in reputation then career outcomes must reflect that ambition. I would start by restructuring Career Services to be profile-centric and industry-mapped, ensuring internships and opportunities are aligned with students’ backgrounds and specialisations. Priority would be given to legacy and credible organisations, and internship stipends would be benchmarked against peer B-schools to meet MBA-level expectations. Even if compensation is limited, brand value and role clarity should be non-negotiable. At the same time, I would streamline Career Prep requirements so they support not burden students. Resume submissions and preparatory modules should be milestone-based rather than repetitive, ensuring effort translates into tangible outcomes.

careerservices_feedback_comment: I genuinely appreciate the effort the outreach team is putting in to bring opportunities to YLC. Their intent and the volume of outreach are evident. However, the quality of many of the opportunities being brought in is concerning. A significant number of roles offer very low stipends, and while the “learning opportunity” justification mentioned during the town hall is valid in some cases, it does not apply uniformly to all the companies being invited. There have been instances where companies with extremely limited operational scale were allowed to recruit. For example, a company named Alcovia reportedly had only one employee at the time of recruitment. This raises serious questions about the screening criteria being used to approve employers. Due to such cases, some of my classmates had their career coach access blocked simply because they chose not to proceed with companies that had minimal credibility or structure. This feels unfair and discouraging. Additionally, job descriptions are often misaligned with the actual roles discussed during interviews. Critical expectations and responsibilities emerge only later, indicating a lack of clarity and proper vetting at the JD stage. It sometimes appears that the focus may be on maximizing the number of companies brought in, potentially to reflect stronger placement statistics, rather than ensuring quality, credibility, and role alignment. This approach risks diluting the overall placement experience. That said, I would like to acknowledge that the career services team members are genuinely supportive, cooperative, and empathetic. Their moral support during the placement process is extremely valuable and appreciated. I sincerely request that employer screening standards be strengthened, with greater emphasis on company credibility, role clarity, and fair compensation, so that the opportunities presented truly align with students’ expectations and long-term career goals.

program_fix_comment: If I were sitting in Pratham’s chair, the first thing I would fix is the overall sentiment around MU within the cohort. Right now, the dominant mood is negative, and that affects engagement, trust, and long-term brand value. I feel this negativity stems from three key areas. 1. Career Services Perception There is a strong perception that the career services team is not putting in enough effort or not adding tangible value. Whether or not that is entirely accurate, perception becomes reality for students. I would start by: Setting clear, measurable career support goals (number of recruiter connects, mock interviews, resume reviews, company outreach updates). Creating a transparent tracker visible to the cohort showing weekly progress. Hosting monthly open forums where the career team shares what they are actively working on and takes direct feedback. Introducing personalized career action plans so students feel individually supported rather than processed in bulk. The goal would be to move from opacity to transparency and from generic support to structured accountability. 2. Quality Over Quantity in Sessions There is currently too much focus on outclass sessions, but very few that add real, actionable value. I would shift the focus from volume to impact: Reduce the number of sessions. Vet speakers more strictly based on relevance and practical insight. Ensure every session has a clear takeaway (framework, template, hiring insight, or actionable skill). If students feel their time is respected, sentiment will improve naturally. 3. Expectation Setting & Brand Positioning Through ads and the admissions process, certain expectations were set. Many students feel there was a gap between what was promised and what was delivered. It feels like overcommit and underdeliver - and that is where trust erodes. I would fundamentally shift the philosophy to: Undercommit and overdeliver. Be realistic during admissions. Show real outcomes, real challenges, real effort required. When you set slightly conservative expectations and then exceed them, that’s when a brand becomes strong and trusted.

program_fix_comment: I would prioritise building a stronger community and streamlining operations, the two areas that directly impact the student experience and organisational sustainability. 1. Fostering Meaningful Connections The first change would be creating intentional touchpoints for community building. Currently, there's untapped potential in cross-cohort interactions. I'd establish: - Cross-program mixers: Regular social events bringing together PGP TBM, YLC, and TETR students during overlapping periods, plus undergraduate students. These connections create mentorship opportunities, knowledge sharing, and a stronger institutional identity. - Direct stakeholder engagement: Students working on backend teams should interact directly with the departments they serve. This breaks down silos, accelerates learning, and ensures solutions are grounded in real user needs rather than assumptions. These initiatives would transform our campus from a collection of separate programs into an integrated ecosystem. 2. Strategic Growth Over Rapid Scaling While I understand we're in a growth phase, the current onboarding pace appears unsustainable. The visible weekly influx of new team members suggests we may be prioritising quantity over quality and integration. I'd advocate for: Slower, more deliberate hiring: Ensuring each hire adds clear value and can be properly absorbed into the culture Quality over speed: Better to have a slightly smaller, highly effective team than an oversized one struggling with coordination This isn't about limiting growth, but about sustainable scaling that maintains organisational effectiveness. 3. Operational Excellence in Onboarding The current onboarding process is too lengthy and bureaucratic. Having experienced it firsthand, I've seen how inefficiencies in the HR/onboarding pipeline create frustration and delay productivity. I'd streamline this by: Process audit: Identifying and eliminating redundant steps Clear timelines: Setting and meeting concrete deadlines Responsive support: Ensuring the onboarding team has the resources and accountability to move quickly First impressions matter. A smooth onboarding experience sets the tone for someone's entire journey with the institution. 4. Practical Student Support Finally, I'd address a concrete pain point: parking for students commuting 15-20+ kilometres. This isn't just about convenience-it's about accessibility and inclusion. Solutions could include reserved spots, subsidised parking passes, or partnerships with nearby facilities. Small investments in daily quality-of-life issues yield significant returns in student satisfaction and retention. In essence, I'd focus on strengthening community bonds, ensuring operational sustainability, and removing friction from the student experience, changes that create lasting value rather than just short-term fixes.

careerservices_feedback_comment: I am extremely dissatisfied with the quality of support provided by the Career Services team. My experience has been consistently disappointing, unprofessional, and far below the expectations set when I joined Masters' Union. First, the general attitude towards students has been disrespectful and dismissive. There is no sense of personalized guidance or genuine interest in students’ career trajectories. Interactions feel transactional, rushed, and often rude. In my personal experience, communication with Siddhartha, Dibyendu was particularly poor, responses were curt, unhelpful, and conveyed a complete lack of professionalism. Second, the quality of internships being sourced is not aligned with what was promised. Students are not opposed to modest stipends, a ₹25,000 stipend is acceptable, provided the opportunity offers meaningful learning, credibility, or brand value. Unfortunately, many of the opportunities being offered fail on all three counts. Third, there appears to be no proper credibility or quality check before onboarding startups. In my interview with Alcovea, it became clear only *after* speaking to the founder that the startup had no funding, no team, and zero full-time employees despite operating for over a year. There was one school-level intern, and unrealistic promises of PPOs were being made to multiple candidates without any structural backing. This raised serious red flags that should have been identified by the Career Services team *before* students were asked to engage. Despite not receiving any official offer letter or post-interview communication from the startup, my placement code was blocked by the Career Services team. This action was taken without explanation and reflects a deeply concerning approach, one that prioritizes marking a student as “placed” over evaluating whether the opportunity is credible, ethical, or beneficial to the student’s long-term career. Overall, the Career Services team appears more focused on closing placements at any cost rather than safeguarding students’ futures. There is little listening, no accountability, and a worrying willingness to push students towards low-quality, poorly vetted roles. I have spoken with alumni and am aware that Career Services support was significantly better in previous years. Unfortunately, the current experience has been so poor that it has genuinely impacted my perception of the institution. This is not feedback I am giving lightly, it comes from repeated negative experiences and a growing sense of regret. I strongly urge the administration to review the conduct, responsiveness, and screening processes of the Career Services team. Students deserve professionalism, transparency, and opportunities that do not compromise their time, credibility, or career growth.

program_fix_comment: If I were sitting in Pratham’s chair, the first and most urgent issue I would address is the Career Services function, specifically, the quality, credibility, and timeliness of internship and placement opportunities. Career Services is one of the most critical touchpoints that directly shapes students’ outcomes, alumni advocacy, and the long-term reputation of the institution. At present, this area is causing significant dissatisfaction among students. It is late February, and many students still do not have credible, high-quality internship opportunities in hand. This creates anxiety and undermines the confidence students placed in the institution. The concern is not about stipend levels. Students are willing to accept modest compensation — even ₹25,000 — as long as the opportunity offers learning value, mentorship, and brand credibility. The real issue is that many roles being offered lack basic verification: startups with unclear funding, no structured teams, unrealistic promises, or limited credibility. Students are not here to experiment with unvetted opportunities at the cost of their careers. A stronger due-diligence framework for onboarding companies, clearer communication, and a more student-centric approach are urgently needed. Career Services should act as a trusted gatekeeper, not just a placement pipeline. Students should feel protected, guided, and supported — not pressured to accept roles that do not align with their professional growth. This issue has broader implications. Word-of-mouth, alumni recommendations, and students’ willingness to advocate for Masters' Union are directly impacted by placement outcomes. At present, Career Services is becoming a deterrent rather than a differentiator. Fixing this would immediately improve student morale, institutional credibility, and long-term brand equity. Strengthening Career Services is not just an operational fix — it is a strategic imperative. If I were in Pratham’s position, I would take immediate and decisive action to overhaul the Career Services function. At this stage, incremental fixes or internal course corrections are not sufficient. Despite multiple rounds of student feedback, concerns around internship quality, credibility of roles, and communication standards have not been addressed with the seriousness they require. This indicates a deeper structural issue rather than a short-term operational gap. My first step would be to completely restructure the Career Services team and bring in experienced professionals from institutions with a proven placement track record — such as leading IIMs — or from strong corporate recruitment backgrounds. Career Services must be led by individuals who understand employer expectations, brand positioning, and student outcomes at a benchmark level. Second, I would personally intervene to rebuild employer relationships. As the founder, I would directly engage with credible companies and established startups to bring them on campus, especially given that this is a C1 YRC batch that will set the precedent for future cohorts. If this batch does not receive strong outcomes, the long-term sustainability and credibility of the YRC program itself is at risk. Currently, the internship cycle is significantly delayed compared to peer institutions, where opportunities were finalized weeks ago. This delay, combined with the inconsistent quality of roles being offered, has created serious concern among students about wasted time and lost career momentum. Career Services should function as a high-accountability unit that listens actively, acts decisively, and treats student careers as long-term investments, not short-term checkboxes. Until that shift happens, this will continue to be one of the most damaging pain points for students and for the brand of Masters' Union. Addressing this immediately would send a strong signal that student outcomes are non-negotiable and that leadership is willing to make tough decisions to protect institutional credibility.

acad_most_impact_comment: Garima Ma’am (Macroeconomics) and Bhupesh Sir (Market Research) had the most impact on me. Garima Ma’am genuinely changed how I look at macroeconomics. She made the subject feel very real and connected it to what’s actually happening in the economy and businesses around us. Her way of explaining concepts made them much easier to understand, and I started following economic news more seriously because of her classes. Bhupesh Sir’s Market Research course had a big impact on how I think. It made me more structured and analytical. I now approach problems by breaking them down properly instead of jumping to conclusions. His classes were very practical, and I feel much more confident working with data and insights because of them. Both of them have shaped the way I think and approach decisions.
acad_feedback_comment: Some of the courses are extremely well designed and have a lot of depth. However, in a few cases, I felt the delivery did not fully do justice to the potential of the subject. For example, Corporate Strategy is such an important and high-impact course, but I feel it could have been taught in a more structured and engaging way. With stronger classroom discussions, more real-world case breakdowns, and clearer frameworks, the learning experience could have been much more powerful. The content itself is strong it’s more about the execution and delivery. Overall, the academic experience has been good, but refining how certain core subjects are taught would make it significantly better.
outclass_content_clarity_comment: Not really. While the challenge was interesting and made me think about content creation more seriously, I still haven’t gained full clarity on my niche, the right platform, or the type of content I should focus on. I do want to pursue it, but I think I’m still figuring out where I naturally fit and what would be sustainable for me long term. I also feel I need more confidence and maybe a bit more structured guidance to take the first step properly.
outclass_feedback_comment: A few of the sessions felt like they could have been more structured and outcome-driven. In some cases, it felt like the time invested did not translate into enough practical value or clarity. Specifically, the Content Creation Challenge and related sessions could be designed much better. I think clearer frameworks, step-by-step guidance, and more practical execution support would make a big difference. Right now, it feels slightly abstract, and many of us are still unsure about how to actually implement what was discussed. With tighter structure and stronger accountability or feedback loops, Outclass could become much more impactful.
careerprep_confidence_comment: Yes, Career Prep has definitely helped me feel more confident overall, especially in terms of understanding how to approach placements. However, I feel the ANM sessions need to be structured better. This term in particular, they didn’t add much value for me and felt slightly unorganized. On the positive side, Gopika’s mock interview sessions were extremely helpful. The feedback was practical and actionable, and it genuinely boosted my confidence. If more sessions could be structured like that focused, personalized, and feedback-driven it would make Career Prep much stronger and more effective.
careerprep_feedback_comment: Overall, Career Prep has been helpful in building direction and awareness around placements. However, I feel there is scope to make the structure more consistent and outcome-driven, especially for sessions like ANM, which this term didn’t feel very impactful. More personalized feedback, structured mock simulations, and clearer roadmaps tailored to different career paths would make the process much stronger. The mock interview support (like the one conducted by Gopika) was extremely valuable more sessions in that format would really help build real confidence. With tighter execution and stronger structure, Career Prep can become one of the most powerful support systems for students.
careerservices_feedback_comment: I appreciate the efforts being put into outreach and building company relationships. However, I feel there could be more transparency and communication around the outreach process for example, clearer updates on which companies are being approached, what stage conversations are at, and what profiles are being targeted. It would also help if outreach were more aligned with student aspirations and strengths, with clearer mapping between roles and student readiness. Regular updates or dashboards could increase confidence in the process. Overall, the intent is strong, but improving communication, visibility, and alignment would make Career Services (Outreach) feel much more structured and reassuring.
masterrelations_guestfavorite_comment: Garima Ma’am, Bhupesh Sir, and Gopika are the sessions I enjoyed the most. Garima Ma’am’s Macroeconomics classes stood out because she made the subject feel very real and connected it to what’s actually happening in the economy and businesses around us. It never felt like just theory it felt relevant and practical. Bhupesh Sir’s Market Research sessions were impactful because they made me think in a much more structured and analytical way. I liked how practical and application-based the learning was. Gopika’s mock interview sessions stood out because the feedback was honest, specific, and actionable. It actually helped me improve and feel more confident rather than just being a formal exercise.
masterrelations_feedback_comment: Some of the CXO sessions felt slightly irrelevant or difficult to relate to from a student perspective. While it’s great to hear from experienced leaders, at times the discussions felt too high-level and not directly connected to our current stage or practical challenges. It would be helpful if sessions were more structured around specific themes, with clearer takeaways and more interactive elements like Q&A or case-based discussions. Making the conversations more grounded in student realities and early-career decision-making would make them much more impactful. The exposure is valuable it just needs better alignment and contextualization to truly resonate.
studentaffairs_value_comment: In Term 3, what added the most value for me was the overall exposure to different perspectives whether through peer discussions, case conversations, or interacting with people who think very differently from me. I felt like this term pushed me to reflect more on what I actually want to do and where I see myself. It wasn’t just about academics, but about becoming more self-aware and clearer about my strengths and gaps. That internal clarity and mindset shift has probably been the most valuable part of the experience this term.
studentaffairs_feedback_comment: Overall, Student Affairs has been supportive, but I feel communication and responsiveness can be improved. At times, important updates or changes feel last-minute, which makes planning difficult from a student’s perspective. It would also help to have a more structured feedback mechanism where student concerns are acknowledged and visibly acted upon. Even small improvements in coordination and proactive communication would make the overall student experience smoother and more reassuring. The intent and effort are visible with tighter execution, it can become much stronger.
tech_careercoach_comment: The Career Coach platform is helpful as a centralized place to apply for internships and jobs, but one major issue is that many roles are not explained clearly. The job descriptions often feel incomplete, which makes it difficult to fully understand the expectations, required skills, or growth opportunities.
tech_mentorunion_comment: I feel adding more mentors across different domains would make it much more effective. Sometimes the availability feels limited, especially if we’re looking for guidance in specific roles or industries.It would also help to make scheduling calls easier and more flexible. A smoother booking system with clearer availability slots would encourage more students to actually use the platform consistently.
tech_lms_comment: sometimes it feels laggy and requires frequent refreshing, which interrupts the flow while accessing content. This can be a bit frustrating, especially during deadlines or when trying to quickly find something.
program_nps_reason_comment: The single biggest reason for my score is that Term 3 felt fine overall there were definitely valuable learning moments and good experiences, but at the same time, a few areas lacked structure and impact. It wasn’t outstanding, but it wasn’t disappointing either. It felt average with some strong highlights.
program_positive_comment: The practical and discussion-driven classes worked best for me this term. Sessions where there were real case studies, interactive conversations, and clear takeaways were the most impactful. They made learning feel relevant and engaging. Mock interviews and feedback-driven sessions also worked really well because they were actionable and directly connected to placements. Continuing with more structured, outcome-focused, and interactive sessions would definitely be beneficial.
program_fix_comment: If I were sitting in Pratham’s chair, I would focus on improving both Academics and Outclass immediately. On the academics side, courses need to feel more relevant and better delivered. Scheduling should be fixed so important classes don’t clash. Also, faculty selection should prioritize teaching ability as much as experience and credibility. A strong profile is important, but the ability to engage, simplify, and structure learning matters even more. With Outclass, there needs to be a lot of changes. Sessions should be more structured, practical, and outcome-driven. Many of them feel abstract and not directly useful. Clear frameworks, execution support, and measurable outcomes would make Outclass much more impactful instead of just theoretical exposure. Overall, tighter structure, better execution, and stronger alignment with student needs would be my immediate focus.

outclass_content_clarity_comment: Not really, we did have session but personally did not feel connected to it. Especially, it is not about picking one niche or platform, most of the people are doing it for more views, so majorly posting content which can get more reach, a lot of students have picked niche as well but then again the purpose of that is to build distribution but then content creation challenge feels more like a rush, focussed towards success or getting positive results like more views etc. The purpose then do gets defeated. Plus, people who are actually doing in a niche are the ones passionate about that, content creation sessions did not have a big impact on that.
outclass_feedback_comment: Yes, firstly we are always told to check the handbook for everything, then why was content creation preponed in the first place in term 3, when it was given for term5/6, thats hypocrisy, we are told to check handbook for all assignments or fair's and then the same is not followed for whatever reason, don't give that reasoning then, when you cannot follow it in the first place. Building on the above point, how does it make sense to have both content creation and vip pre seed together and then mvp as well, although it does help us build distribution but then posting 3 reels every week, that limit does not make sense. We would want to do one full heartedly, then everything averagely. Now for MVP, there was MVP Kick Off somewhere around 15th Jan, we were given generic info about what is MVP and how in earlier times companies have built MVP. Nothing was mentioned about MVP Fair, and now all of sudden 2 weeks later assignments are posted for MVP, in which by 27th Feb, you need to have your products, and you need to be post revenue. How does it make sense to be post revenue, when you are literally building your MVP, you are trying to test your product, thats what MVP is for, post revenue does not make sense, and for people who are genuinely building and trying to get the best product made and launch else it affects them in their long term, then these deadlines forces them to either get a bad product or have long term effects on business, especially one building in luxury or pure tech products. And then we are told it's your problem because it was given in handbook, how does that make sense?? Because we won't refer to handbook for everything, especially when you are the one not following it. Secondly, business are not built on deadlines, agreed it help us to launch soon but then we also would want to launch soon rather then die out in early stage of the business. Hence, I think the entire outclass needs to be re-looked, because that is MU's Moat, else it may crumble.

program_fix_comment: As members of the founding batch of YLC students, we understand that we are setting the standard and shaping the perception of the program for future cohorts. When prospective students reach out to us to understand the overall college experience and course structure, I usually share a very positive outlook because I am genuinely satisfied with most aspects of the program. However, when conversations shift toward internships and career opportunities, I often find myself less confident in my response. While the job-related packages for TBM seem promising, the internship opportunities currently available to us do not feel equally satisfactory. This word-of-mouth feedback inevitably influences how the program is perceived externally. If this area is not strengthened, it may affect future interest and credibility. It would be extremely beneficial if the institution could introduce more focused sessions on improving student capabilities, real-world application of learning, and structured guidance on securing stronger internship roles, especially those in the ₹70,000–₹80,000 range or higher. Strengthening this aspect would not only support current students but also enhance the overall reputation of the YLC program.
